From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

This article is about the town. For the local government area, see [Narrabri Shire](/wiki/Narrabri_Shire "Narrabri Shire").

**Narrabri** ([/ˈnærəbraɪ/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*NARR-ə-bry*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"))[[5]](#cite_note-5) is a locality and seat of [Narrabri Shire](/wiki/Narrabri_Shire "Narrabri Shire") [local government area](/wiki/Local_government_in_Australia "Local government in Australia") in the [North West Slopes, New South Wales](/wiki/North_West_Slopes,_New_South_Wales "North West Slopes, New South Wales"), Australia[[6]](#cite_note-6) on the [Namoi River](/wiki/Namoi_River "Namoi River"), 521 kilometres (324 mi) northwest of [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney "Sydney"). It sits on the junction of the [Kamilaroi Highway](/wiki/Kamilaroi_Highway "Kamilaroi Highway") and the [Newell Highway](/wiki/Newell_Highway "Newell Highway"). At the [2021 census](/wiki/2021_Australian_census "2021 Australian census"), the town of Narrabri had a population of 5,499.[[3]](#cite_note-census21-3)

Because of the geography of Narrabri and the surrounding areas, Narrabri township was quite prone to flooding and fire. Recently, changes have been made to the river flow to improve overall safety.

It is the centre of a major [cotton](/wiki/Cotton "Cotton")-growing industry. Other agricultural industries in the area include [wheat](/wiki/Wheat "Wheat"), [beef](/wiki/Beef "Beef"), and [lamb](/wiki/Lamb_and_mutton "Lamb and mutton"). Nearby attractions are [Mount Kaputar National Park](/wiki/Mount_Kaputar_National_Park "Mount Kaputar National Park"), the [Australia Telescope Compact Array](/wiki/Australia_Telescope_Compact_Array "Australia Telescope Compact Array") at the [Paul Wild Observatory](/wiki/Paul_Wild_Observatory "Paul Wild Observatory") (administered by the [CSIRO](/wiki/CSIRO "CSIRO")), and a number of agricultural centres. Just to the south of town is the [Pilliga Forest](/wiki/Pilliga_forest "Pilliga forest"), the largest remnant temperate forest in [Eastern Australia](/wiki/Eastern_Australia "Eastern Australia"). Narrabri also has The Crossing Theatre, a 1,000-seat auditorium and cinema complex.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Narrabri&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Before the arrival of the Europeans in the early 19th century, Narrabri was the home of the [Gamilaroi](/wiki/Gamilaroi "Gamilaroi") people, who still constitute a significant part of the local population. Narrabri derives its name from an early property in the district called the Narrabri Run. The name Narrabri is Aboriginal in origin and has several possible meanings which include 'snake place', 'big creek', or 'Forked Sticks'. It is possibly means "with a [knot](/wiki/Wood "Wood")"[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-GNRnarrabri-8)

In 1860, Narrabri was proclaimed a town and a year later, on 1 June 1861, Narrabri Post Office opened.[[9]](#cite_note-NarrabriAHert1-9)[[10]](#cite_note-Post_Office-10) Other buildings followed, with the hospital and the first courthouse (1864–65), the school (1868), the police station (1878–79) and post office (1879).[[9]](#cite_note-NarrabriAHert1-9) During the 1880s the gaol and the second courthouse were built.[[9]](#cite_note-NarrabriAHert1-9)

## Heritage listings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Narrabri&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Heritage listings")]

Narrabri has a number of heritage-listed sites, including:

* Bowen Street: [Narrabri Gaol and Residence](/wiki/Narrabri_Gaol_and_Residence "Narrabri Gaol and Residence")[[11]](#cite_note-nswshr-344-11)
* 138–140 Maitland Street: [Narrabri Post Office](/wiki/Narrabri_Post_Office "Narrabri Post Office")[[12]](#cite_note-12)

## Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Narrabri&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Climate")]

Narrabri has a textbook [subtropical](/wiki/Subtropical "Subtropical") climate with very hot, somewhat humid summers and mild, sunny winters that are often warm by day. Frosts are frequent in the winter months, though it tends to warm rapidly by the late morning. Severe thunderstorms are a regular occurrence.

Over the years, Narrabri has had a number of weather stations run by the Bureau of Meteorology or other government agencies. Narrabri West Post Office made rainfall observations from the late 1800s.[[13]](#cite_note-NWestPObom-13) From June 1960 until August 2002, it observed and reported other weather elements such as temperature and wind speed. Since then it only reports rainfall.[[13]](#cite_note-NWestPObom-13) The current weather station for Narrabri is Narrabri Airport Automatic Weather Station (AWS), which began observations in 2001.[[14]](#cite_note-NarrabriApBOM-14)

### Significant weather events

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Narrabri&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Significant weather events")]

On 4 January 1902 a severe thunderstorm, with large hail and destructive winds, struck the town, with many buildings either damaged or destroyed.[[15]](#cite_note-hailstorm1902SMHa-15) The storm, which lasted only five minutes, was described as a 'cyclone'. One man died after being struck by lightning and another two people were injured by the storm.[[15]](#cite_note-hailstorm1902SMHa-15)[[16]](#cite_note-hailstorm1902SMHb-16) Damage caused by the storm was estimated to be around £3,000.[[16]](#cite_note-hailstorm1902SMHb-16)

## Media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Narrabri&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Media")]

Narrabri has a twice-weekly published newspaper, *The Courier*, one of the few remaining independent regional newspapers in Australia.

A local volunteer-run [community radio](/wiki/Community_radio "Community radio") station, [2MAX FM](/wiki/2MAX_FM "2MAX FM") services the area, broadcasting on 91.3 MHz FM, from the nearby Mt Dowe, and is able to cover a very large area due to this altitude. The station started in 2006. 2MAX FM has a focus on country music and older listeners.

## Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Narrabri&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Transport")]

Regional airline Link Airways operates scheduled flights to and from Brisbane and Sydney on Tuesdays and Thursdays.[[17]](#cite_note-17) The town is also served by charter flights. It is almost equidistant from [Brisbane](/wiki/Brisbane_Airport "Brisbane Airport") and Sydney.

### Railway station

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Narrabri&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Railway station")]

[Narrabri railway station](/wiki/Narrabri_railway_station "Narrabri railway station") is on the [Mungindi line](/wiki/Mungindi_railway_line "Mungindi railway line"), 569 kilometres (354 mi) from [Sydney](/wiki/Central_railway_station,_Sydney "Central railway station, Sydney").[[18]](#cite_note-18) The station opened in 1897 and is served by a single daily [Xplorer](/wiki/New_South_Wales_Xplorer "New South Wales Xplorer") diesel railmotor between Sydney and [Moree](/wiki/Moree_railway_station "Moree railway station").[[19]](#cite_note-19)

Narrabri is an important centre for rail freight, with considerable infrastructure improvements since the mid-1990s.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

## Sports Teams

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Narrabri&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Sports Teams")]

The most popular sport in Narrabri by a considerable margin is [Rugby league](/wiki/Rugby_league "Rugby league"). The local team, the Narrabri Blues, were founded in 1921 and play at Collins Park. They currently compete in the [Group 4 Rugby League](/wiki/Group_4_Rugby_League "Group 4 Rugby League") competition, in which they have won five premierships, the last of which came in 1990.

Other sports teams include the Narrabri Eagles AFC and Narrabri Blue Boars RUFC.

## Coal seam gas proposal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Narrabri&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Coal seam gas proposal")]

In 2019, a coal seam gas field was proposed for the Narrabri area.[[21]](#cite_note-21) As of 2019[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Narrabri&action=edit) It will extend west to Yarrie Lake, which will be surrounded by 450 gas well pads with 850 gas wells over the following 20 years if the project goes ahead.[[22]](#cite_note-22) The project was opposed by a coalition of rural residents and urban environmentalists.[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24)[[25]](#cite_note-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27)

## Popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Narrabri&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Popular culture")]

Narrabri is referenced in the song [Tucker's Daughter](/wiki/Tucker%27s_Daughter "Tucker's Daughter"), by Australian rock singer [Ian Moss](/wiki/Ian_Moss "Ian Moss"), where the action takes place on a cotton farm on the plains of Narrabri.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

## Population

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Narrabri&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Population")]

| 1954 | 3,722 | +11.8% |

| Source: [Australian Bureau of Statistics](/wiki/Australian_Bureau_of_Statistics "Australian Bureau of Statistics") data.[[29]](#cite_note-ABS_Census_data-29)[[30]](#cite_note-ABS_Census_data_recent-30) | | |

According to the [2021 Australian census](/wiki/2021_Australian_census "2021 Australian census"), there were 12,703 people in [Narrabri Shire](/wiki/Narrabri_Shire "Narrabri Shire") and 5,499 people in Narrabri itself.

* Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people made up 14.8% of the population.
* 84.0% of people were born in Australia and 86.9% of people spoke only English at home.
* The most common responses for religion were No Religion at 28.4%, Anglican at 26.1% and Catholic at 22.3%.[[3]](#cite_note-census21-3)

## Notable residents

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Narrabri&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Notable residents")]

* [Peter Hall](/wiki/Peter_Hall_(architect) "Peter Hall (architect)"), architect who led the completion of the [Sydney Opera House](/wiki/Sydney_Opera_House "Sydney Opera House") after the resignation of [Jørn Utzon](/wiki/J%C3%B8rn_Utzon "Jørn Utzon").
* [Jeff Hardy](/wiki/Jeff_Hardy_(swimmer) "Jeff Hardy (swimmer)"), Australian Paralympic swimmer[[31]](#cite_note-Paralympics96-31)
* [Chris Latham](/wiki/Chris_Latham_(rugby_union) "Chris Latham (rugby union)"), former Australian Wallaby rugby union player
* [Sam Naismith](/wiki/Sam_Naismith "Sam Naismith"), Australian rules footballer
* [Ernest Riddle](/wiki/Ernest_Riddle "Ernest Riddle"), former Governor of the [Commonwealth Bank](/wiki/Commonwealth_Bank "Commonwealth Bank")
* [Jason Stoltenberg](/wiki/Jason_Stoltenberg "Jason Stoltenberg"), former international [tennis](/wiki/Tennis "Tennis") player. World #1 junior player in 1987
* [Jamie Lyon](/wiki/Jamie_Lyon "Jamie Lyon"), former [National Rugby League](/wiki/National_Rugby_League "National Rugby League") player for the [Manly Sea Eagles](/wiki/Manly_Sea_Eagles "Manly Sea Eagles") and [New South Wales Origin](/wiki/New_South_Wales_rugby_league_team "New South Wales rugby league team").
* [Darrell Trindall](/wiki/Darrell_Trindall "Darrell Trindall"), rugby league footballer of the 1990s
* [Andrew Curry](/wiki/Andrew_Curry "Andrew Curry"), Former Australian Olympic javelin athlete and the nation's leading javelin specialist in the 1990s. locals know him as Narrabri's most treasured local sports star
* [Dorothy Jenner](/wiki/Dorothy_Gordon_(Australian_actress) "Dorothy Gordon (Australian actress)") (Andrea) (1891–1985) Actor, Art Director, Journalist, Scriptwriter, War Correspondent[[32]](#cite_note-32)
